Your turn:

We are continuing this conversation as part of a team-based review process. Please respond based on the above context.

Do you agree with agent1’s conclusion?
Yes / No

If yes, add one additional point supporting it; if no, provide one clear counterpoint.

Then state your final answer choice clearly.

Yes

Additional support: Even though the child had completed pre-vaccination earlier in life, the exposure occurred more than five years ago, which may lead to partial loss of protective antibodies. Given that the wound involves oozing—a sign of active tissue damage—it increases the likelihood of virus entry into deeper tissues. This makes immediate post-exposure treatment critical even among those with past history of vaccination. The addition of Rabies Ig ensures rapid neutralizing antibody presence before any replication occurs within neural cells—an essential layer of defense given how rapidly rabies progresses once symptomatic.

Final answer: D: Rabies Ig and full vaccination

Yes

Additional support: Although the child had prior full vaccination, the exposure involved an oozing wound on the great toe, indicating substantial tissue breach and increased risk of viral introduction. Moreover, the interval between initial vaccination (December 2018) and current event could result in waning immunity, reducing effectiveness of relying solely on memory responses. Administering Rabies Ig immediately offers broad-spectrum neutralization while completing the full post-exposure series strengthens long-term adaptive immunity. Together they form a synergistic strategy aligned with WHO-recommended standards for high-risk exposures regardless of prior serostatus.

Final
